original_hypothesis.title: Public distribution start can be used to intentionally freeze all transfers by forcing a distribution that cannot complete (gas-bound lock)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Any account can start distribution once the period elapses, which immediately sets `LockedForDistribution=true` and blocks all transfers/mints/burns. If the holders set is large enough, the distribution cannot be completed quickly, turning the protocol into a transfer-frozen state for an extended time and potentially indefinitely if completion becomes economically infeasible.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Locking is a global circuit breaker triggered by a public function. Combined with holder-array bloat (by normal usage or adversarial duplication), an unprivileged actor can deterministically force a freeze without any transaction-ordering advantage.

proof_of_signal.pattern: distribute(uint256) is public; when starting, _beginDistribution() sets LockedForDistribution=true before iterating; completion requires iterating through `holders`

preconditions: holders array is large enough (or contains many phantom/duplicate entries) such that completing distribution requires many calls
Attackers can pay gas for at least one distribute call to trigger lock

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Global lock is engaged at distribution start, but distribution completion is multi-transaction and depends on bounded iteration.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever MinDistributionPeriod has elapsed; attacker can choose worst operational moments (e.g., during maintenance) without mempool.

operational_questions: What is the maximum holders size for which distribution can be completed within acceptable time/cost?
What monitoring/automation exists to rapidly complete distributions once started?
Is there a fallback/unlock mechanism if distribution cannot complete?
validation_requirements: Simulate worst-case `holders` growth and measure number of distribute() calls required
Check whether incomplete distribution can be rescued if some token transfers revert/return false mid-loop (depends on reward token behavior)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait until MinDistributionPeriod has elapsed (observable on-chain).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call `distribute(1)` to start distribution and lock transfers.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Do nothing further (or intermittently call distribute with minimal batch sizes) so others must bear cost to unlock by completing distribution.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat each period to maximize downtime.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Global transfer freeze during distribution; mint/burn blocked; users cannot exit positions via transfers
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium to high (requires executing many distribute calls; if economically prohibitive, may require redeploy/migration)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All ERC20 transfers and supply operations

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Triggered purely by on-chain state; no ordering assumptions

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: DistributionStarted emitted by unknown account; long gaps before DistributionFinished
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Spike in failed user transfers with 'distribution in progress'
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Attacker repeatedly starting distributions with tiny batch sizes

validation_result.mechanism: permissionless global lock via public distribute()
validation_result.impact_summary: Any account can trigger a global transfer/mint/burn freeze by calling distribute(1) once MinDistributionPeriod elapses. The freeze persists until all holders are iterated through, which can be made arbitrarily expensive via the address(0) accumulation bug in _beforeTokenTransfer.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies a real code defect. The distribute() function is public (no access control beyond nonReentrant) and immediately sets LockedForDistribution=true via _beginDistribution() before processing any holders. Once locked, _beforeTokenTransfer reverts ALL transfers, mints, and burns. The only way to unlock is to complete the entire distribution by iterating through every entry in the holders array. There is no emergency unlock, no abort mechanism, and no owner override. An attacker can call distribute(1) to engage the lock while processing only a single holder, forcing others to bear the cost of completing the distribution. This is compounded by INV-STATE-001: every burn pushes address(0) into the holders array, meaning the array grows unboundedly with phantom entries. With enough address(0) entries, completing a distribution becomes prohibitively expensive or impossible within block gas limits, creating a permanent freeze.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code clearly shows: (1) distribute() is public with no access control at LiquidInfrastructureERC20.sol, (2) _beginDistribution() sets LockedForDistribution=true before any iteration, (3) _beforeTokenTransfer requires !LockedForDistribution for ALL token operations, (4) _endDistribution() is only reachable when nextDistributionRecipient == holders.length, (5) there is no alternative path to set LockedForDistribution=false, and (6) the holders array can grow unboundedly due to address(0) accumulation from burns.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Public function with no access control that immediately engages a global lock via _beginDistribution(). Caller controls numDistributions parameter, so can process as few as 1 holder while locking the entire contract. No abort/cancel mechanism exists.
Sets LockedForDistribution=true immediately. This is called from distribute() which is public, so any account can trigger this global lock.

ALL transfers, mints, and burns are blocked when LockedForDistribution is true. Additionally, burns push address(0) into holders (balanceOf(address(0)) is always 0), causing unbounded array growth that makes completing distributions increasingly expensive.
This is the ONLY way to set LockedForDistribution=false. It's only called when nextDistributionRecipient == holders.length (all holders processed). There is no emergency unlock or owner override.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker waits until _isPastMinDistributionPeriod() returns true (block.number - LastDistribution >= MinDistributionPeriod, totalSupply > 0, holders.length > 0)
2. Attacker calls distribute(1) - public function, no access control
4. _beginDistribution() sets LockedForDistribution = true, calculates erc20EntitlementPerUnit, sets nextDistributionRecipient = 0
5. distribute processes min(0+1, holders.length) = 1 holder only
6. Since nextDistributionRecipient (1) != holders.length (N), _endDistribution() is NOT called
7. Contract is now locked: all transfers, mints, burns revert with 'distribution in progress'
8. To unlock, someone must call distribute() enough times to iterate through ALL holders entries (including address(0) phantoms)
9. Attacker can repeat this every MinDistributionPeriod blocks to maximize downtime
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: distribute(1) -> _beginDistribution() sets LockedForDistribution=true -> processes only 1 holder -> does NOT call _endDistribution() -> contract remains locked. All subsequent transfer/mint/burn calls hit require(!LockedForDistribution) in _beforeTokenTransfer and revert. The only unlock path requires processing ALL remaining holders.length - 1 entries through additional distribute() calls.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script shows MinDistributionPeriod = 10 blocks (contract-deployer.ts line deploying LiquidInfrastructureERC20 with parameter 10). This is a very short period, meaning the attack window opens every ~10 blocks (~30 seconds on many chains).

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is fully validated: (1) distribute() is public - confirmed at the function signature, no onlyOwner or other access modifier. (2) _beginDistribution() immediately locks the contract - confirmed by LockedForDistribution = true. (3) The lock blocks ALL token operations - confirmed by require(!LockedForDistribution) in _beforeTokenTransfer. (4) Completion requires iterating through all holders - confirmed by the condition nextDistributionRecipient == holders.length to reach _endDistribution(). (5) The holders array can be bloated with address(0) entries from burns - confirmed by _beforeTokenTransfer pushing to=address(0) when balanceOf(address(0))==0. (6) There is no emergency unlock - confirmed by searching all code paths that modify LockedForDistribution.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. An unprivileged attacker can deterministically freeze all ERC20 token operations (transfers, mints, burns) by calling a single public function. The freeze duration depends on the holders array size and willingness of others to pay gas to complete the distribution. With the address(0) accumulation bug, the array can grow to a size where completing distribution is economically infeasible, creating a potentially permanent freeze. This affects all token holders who cannot transfer, sell, or burn their positions during the lock.
recommended_mitigations: Add access control to distribute() so only the owner or an authorized operator can start distributions (or at minimum, add a minimum batch size requirement to prevent distribute(1) griefing)
Add an emergency unlock function restricted to the owner that can abort an in-progress distribution and set LockedForDistribution=false
Fix the address(0) accumulation bug in _beforeTokenTransfer by adding a guard: if (to != address(0)) before the holders.push(to) logic
Add an upper bound on the holders array size to prevent unbounded growth
Consider using a pull-based distribution model (claim pattern) instead of a push-based model to avoid the global lock entirely
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LiquidInfrastructureERC20 with MinDistributionPeriod=10 and a distributable ERC20. 2. Approve multiple holders and mint tokens to them. 3. Perform initial distribution. 4. Perform many burns to bloat holders array with address(0) entries. 5. Wait for MinDistributionPeriod blocks. 6. From a non-owner account, call distribute(1). 7. Verify LockedForDistribution is true. 8. Attempt transfer from any holder - verify it reverts with 'distribution in progress'. 9. Measure gas cost to complete distribution via multiple distribute() calls. 10. Repeat steps 5-6 after distribution completes to show the attack is repeatable.
uncertainties: The practical impact depends on the deployment chain's block gas limit and the actual number of holders (including address(0) phantoms)
Economic feasibility of completing distributions depends on gas prices and the number of holders entries to process
In practice, the owner or an automated system may quickly complete distributions, mitigating the freeze duration - but there is no guarantee of this and no on-chain enforcement
additional_testing_needed: Measure maximum holders array size before distributeToAllHolders() exceeds block gas limit on target chain
Simulate gas costs for partial distribute() calls with various holders array sizes including address(0) entries
Test the interaction between this vulnerability and the address(0) accumulation bug to quantify worst-case freeze duration
Verify that no off-chain automation or monitoring exists that could mitigate the attack
